one_line_takeaway: An unsupervised post-hoc LLM calibration paper (not AI security) that diagnoses post-training-induced overconfidence as two distinct phenomena -- confidence drift and process drift, located via a new Peak Divergence Layer / Inferential Stability Entropy analysis -- and fixes both with a single dynamically-weighted temperature-scaling objective, cutting ECE from double digits to roughly 2-9% across Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5 (7B-32B), and Gemma-3-27B, beating the prior unsupervised SOTA (DACA) and nearing a labeled-data oracle.
key_finding: On MMLU with Llama-3.1-8B, Dual-Align reduces ECE to 2.871% (±0.308), versus 7.811% (±0.619) for the strongest unsupervised baseline DACA and 10.806% (±0.275) for the uncalibrated (Vanilla) model, closely approaching the supervised TS oracle's 1.526% (±0.450). The paper reports this as a >30% relative ECE reduction over strong baselines across architectures (abstract claim). Corresponding MCE/ACE/Brier for Llama-3.1-8B: Dual-Align 5.587%/3.222%/0.445 vs DACA 13.824%/8.064%/0.451 vs Vanilla 18.602%/11.809%/0.461. On Qwen2.5-14B: Dual-Align ECE 2.423% vs DACA 5.146% vs Vanilla 16.735% (TS oracle 2.297%). On Gemma-3-27B: Dual-Align ECE 5.247% vs DACA 16.842% vs Vanilla 23.842% (TS oracle 5.225%). Scaling study (Table 2, Qwen2.5 7B/14B/32B) shows Dual-Align beats DACA at all three sizes (e.g., 14B: ECE 2.423% vs DACA 5.146% vs Vanilla 23.842%; 7B: ECE 9.406% vs DACA 10.312% vs Vanilla 20.666%; 32B: ECE 9.203% vs DACA 10.958% vs Vanilla 11.338%). Ablations (Table 3, Llama-3.1-8B/MMLU) show the confidence-only loss variant is actually worse than DACA (ECE 10.267% vs DACA's 7.811%), process-only reaches ECE 6.082%, a 'simple stratify' variant (confidence loss on agreement samples, process loss on disagreement samples) reaches ECE 5.547%, while the full dynamically-weighted dual loss reaches ECE 2.871% -- demonstrating the per-sample dynamic weighting, not just having both loss terms, drives the gain. A layer-selection ablation (Table 4) shows the dynamic Peak Divergence Layer choice (ECE 2.382%) outperforms fixed-depth alternatives at L/4 (4.716%), L/2 (4.862%), and 3L/4 (2.846%). The method is also shown to generalize to TruthfulQA (open-ended generation via p(true) reformulation) and to models post-trained with PPO, DPO, and GRPO, in each case outperforming DACA and Vanilla (reported via Figure 4, not tabulated numerically in the extracted text).
core_contribution: The paper diagnoses two distinct causes of LLM confidence miscalibration introduced by post-training (instruction tuning/RLHF/etc.): 'confidence drift' (final confidence inflates even though intermediate layer-wise decision processes stay consistent with the pre-trained model, on samples where post-trained and pre-trained models agree) and 'process drift' (intermediate inference pathways diverge sharply at a specific layer, on samples where the two models disagree). It introduces two new diagnostic constructs to make this concrete: the Peak Divergence Layer (PDL), the layer with the largest jump in per-layer Jensen-Shannon divergence between pre-trained and post-trained model logit distributions, and Inferential Stability Entropy (ISE), an entropy computed over per-layer logit-derived weights from the PDL onward that measures how 'rigid' vs. gradual the model's layer-wise conviction is. Building on this diagnosis, the paper proposes Dual-Align, an unsupervised post-hoc calibration method that learns a single temperature parameter via a per-sample-weighted combination of (1) a confidence-alignment KL loss (matching final-layer output distributions to the pre-trained model, following the same mechanism as prior work DACA) and (2) a new process-alignment loss (squared difference between the post-trained and pre-trained model's ISE after the PDL). The per-sample mixing weight is the magnitude of the JSD jump at the PDL, so samples with large process drift are weighted more toward the process-alignment loss and vice versa. The paper also gives an informal theoretical proposition (Proposition 1) bounding a calibration surrogate error by the ISE mismatch term, offered as an explanation for why process alignment helps.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a model-calibration/uncertainty-quantification paper, not a security paper. It contains no adversary, no attack surface, and no security-relevant threat model; it treats post-training-induced overconfidence as a statistical/behavioral phenomenon to correct, not an exploit.
limitations: Authors' stated limitation: the method calibrates by learning a temperature conditioned on predictions from a fixed pre-trained reference LLM; they flag exploring alternative model references (e.g., multimodal models) or training-based (rather than purely post-hoc) calibration as future work. Additional limitations evident from the text: the method requires the specific pre-trained checkpoint that a given post-trained model was derived from to be available at calibration time (though not at inference time); the diagnostic framework (PDL/ISE) and main results are built around multiple-choice QA and require a reformulation trick (p(true)) to extend to open-ended generation; the MCE metric is noisy/inconsistent across ablations (e.g., in Table 2 Dual-Align's MCE on Qwen2.5-14B, 11.241%, is actually worse than DACA's 8.867% despite Dual-Align's better ECE), which the paper does not discuss or explain. Proposition 1 is explicitly labeled 'informal' and relies on a smooth calibration surrogate approximation (logit-gap-based confidence proxy) rather than being a guarantee on the true ECE metric used in experiments.

Llama-3.1-8B/MMLU: Dual-Align MCE 5.587% vs DACA 13.824% vs Vanilla 18.602% vs TS oracle 4.790%
Llama-3.1-8B/MMLU: Dual-Align ACE 3.222% vs DACA 8.064% vs Vanilla 11.809% vs TS oracle 1.985%
Llama-3.1-8B/MMLU: Dual-Align Brier 0.445 vs DACA 0.451 vs Vanilla 0.461 vs TS oracle 0.441
Qwen2.5-14B/MMLU: Dual-Align ECE 2.423% vs DACA 5.146% vs Vanilla 16.735% vs TS oracle 2.297%
Gemma-3-27B/MMLU: Dual-Align ECE 5.247% vs DACA 16.842% vs Vanilla 23.842% vs TS oracle 5.225%

Qwen2.5 scaling (Table 2), ECE: 7B Dual-Align 9.406% vs DACA 10.312% vs Vanilla 20.666%; 14B Dual-Align 2.423% vs DACA 5.146% vs Vanilla 23.842%; 32B Dual-Align 9.203% vs DACA 10.958% vs Vanilla 11.338%
Ablation (Table 3, Llama-3.1-8B/MMLU) ECE: Conf-only 10.267% (worse than DACA 7.811%), Process-only 6.082%, Simple-Stratify 5.547%, Full Dual-Align 2.871%, TS oracle 1.526%
Layer-selection ablation (Table 4, Llama-3.1-8B/MMLU) ECE: fixed L/4 4.716%, L/2 4.862%, 3L/4 2.846%, dynamic PDL (ours) 2.382%, TS oracle 1.526%
Abstract-level summary claim: Dual-Align reduces ECE by more than 30% across various LLM architectures versus strong baselines
baselines_compared: DACA (prior unsupervised PLM-PoLM alignment on agreement samples)
Temperature Scaling (TS, supervised oracle with labeled data)
